section_title: The 4 Acknowledged Failures — 3 Refined, 1 Falsified, and a 5th That Doesn't Count

fingerprints.fingerprint: Polaris prediction function takes WGS84 latitude as axiomatic input
fingerprints.dome_value: predict_polaris_excess(lat) = 0.27 * (lat / 35.9) * exp(-((lat - 35.9)² / 1000)) — predicts a tiny EXCESS above the globe baseline
fingerprints.match_precision: The dome's prediction IS the globe answer plus a 0.27° perturbation
fingerprints.why_it_matters: The entire Polaris prediction framework takes the globe result (elevation ≈ latitude) as ground truth. The dome doesn't derive Polaris elevation from its own geometry (flat disc + Polaris at 6,500 km). If it did, the formula would be arctan(6500/d) where d is horizontal distance from pole — producing a smooth function of disc-radial distance, not of 'latitude.' Instead, the code uses WGS84 latitude directly, measuring the dome's contribution as a tiny residual on top of the globe's answer. The prediction literally cannot exist without the globe framework providing the baseline.

code_search.notes: Function is pure curve-fitting with no connection to dome geometry parameters (disc_radius, polaris_height=6500 km). The 0.27° and 35.9° are fitted constants, not derived from any dome equation.

fingerprints.suggested_text: The dome's Polaris prediction function (predict_polaris_excess in monitor.py) takes WGS84 latitude as its input and predicts a 0.27° excess at 35.9°N — treating the globe's prediction (Polaris elevation ≈ latitude) as axiomatic. The dome never derives Polaris elevation from its own parameters; it measures a tiny perturbation on top of the globe baseline.

fingerprints.fingerprint: Moon angular diameter reframed from dome prediction (local altitude) to globe measurement (apogee/perigee orbital variation)
fingerprints.globe_value: Moon perigee/apogee angular diameter variation: ~14% (orbital distance 356,500–406,700 km)
fingerprints.dome_value: monitor.py line 870-878: predicted 11-14% variation, observed 12.5%, sourced from 'NASA JPL Horizons (OPEN-007 static win)'
fingerprints.match_precision: Predicted=11-14%, Observed=12.5% — matches because the prediction was reframed to match the globe-framework measurement
fingerprints.why_it_matters: The dome's original W021 predicted >2% moonrise-to-transit variation as a consequence of a local moon at 2,534 km altitude. That geometry actually produces ~80% variation (catastrophic failure). The current monitor.py entry tracks apogee/perigee variation instead — a measurement that requires the moon to be at ~384,000 km in a Keplerian orbit around the Earth. The data source (NASA JPL Horizons) is an ephemeris computed using globe-framework orbital mechanics. The dome silently replaced a dome-geometry prediction with a globe-framework measurement and now calls it a pass.

code_search.found_in: monitor.py lines 870-878: Moon Angular Diameter Variation entry; parameters.json: moon_altitude=2534 km
code_search.notes: parameters.json says moon_altitude=2534 km ('V48 triangulation'). But monitor.py uses apogee/perigee variation (a globe orbital concept, not local altitude), sourced from NASA JPL Horizons. The two frameworks are geometrically incompatible.

fingerprints.suggested_text: The dome's moon angular diameter monitoring (monitor.py entry 36) now tracks apogee/perigee variation (11–14%, sourced from NASA JPL Horizons) — a globe-framework orbital measurement. The dome's own parameters place the moon at 2,534 km altitude, which would produce ~80% moonrise-to-transit variation. The prediction was silently reframed from dome geometry (local moon) to globe orbital mechanics (distant moon), and the pass/fail check uses globe-computed ephemeris data.

fingerprints.fingerprint: Roaring 40s prediction depends on globe-specific meteorological phenomena
fingerprints.globe_value: Roaring 40s: persistent westerly winds at 40–60°S latitude, driven by Coriolis deflection on a rotating sphere. 500 hPa geopotential height: defined relative to WGS84 reference geoid.
fingerprints.dome_value: W020: 500hPa geopotential height anomaly ≥3% above climatology in the 40–60°S band
fingerprints.match_precision: N/A — the prediction invokes a globe-framework phenomenon
fingerprints.why_it_matters: On the dome's flat disc, 40–60°S maps to a ring at disc radius 14,400–16,700 km. There is no physical mechanism in the dome model to produce persistent westerly winds in a ring at that radius — the Roaring 40s exist because of Coriolis deflection on a rotating sphere. The concept of '500 hPa geopotential height' is defined relative to the WGS84 geoid (a globe reference surface). The dome is predicting the behavior of a phenomenon that only exists in globe physics, using a measurement framework that only exists in globe geodesy.

code_search.found_in: Not found in code — W020 is not in the current monitor.py audit loop (no live fetch). The prediction was tested manually against NOAA AAO data.
code_search.notes: The absence from monitor.py is itself notable: the dome cannot automate a test for a prediction whose underlying phenomenon requires globe physics to define.

section_level_finding: Section 6.5 documents the dome's failure accounting accurately but misses a pattern: every failed prediction was tested against globe-framework measurements (WGS84 latitude for Polaris, spherical coordinates for NMP drift, geoid-referenced geopotential for Roaring 40s, globe orbital mechanics for moon diameter). The dome's failure-tracking infrastructure is as globe-dependent as its success-tracking infrastructure. The reframing of W021 (moon angular diameter) from a dome-geometry prediction to a globe-orbital measurement is particularly damning: the dome acknowledges a failure by silently switching to the globe framework and calling it a pass.
opportunity: A 1–2 sentence addition to Section 6.5's concluding paragraph could note that the dome's failure-detection framework is itself globe-dependent, reinforcing the broader theme of Section 6.4 (Globe Values Relabeled as Dome Predictions). The moon diameter reframing deserves a specific callout, perhaps with a cross-reference to WIN-021's detail panel.
